section.title: The Song of the Vineyard
section.sectionRationale: This movement opens with a love song turned lawsuit, God's meticulous care for His vineyard, Israel, answered only with wild grapes, and moves into a series of six woes naming specific sins, greed, drunkenness, moral inversion, self-conceit, corrupt justice, until the chapter closes with a foreign army summoned as the LORD's own instrument of judgment. It is the seam where general indictment becomes an itemized charge sheet.
section.thesis: The LORD lavished everything on His vineyard, Israel, expecting justice and righteousness, but found only bloodshed and the cry of the oppressed, so He names their specific sins one by one and summons a swift, relentless army to execute the judgment His patience has finally exhausted.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the song of the vineyard and its bitter fruit; six woes naming Israel's specific sins; and the LORD's whistle for a distant nation, summoned like a lion to devour.

units.label: The song of the vineyard
units.note: wild grapes instead of good fruit

verses.text:
I will sing for my beloved a song of his vineyard: 
My beloved had a {{kerem|vineyard}} on a very fertile hill.

He dug it up and cleared the stones and planted the finest vines. He built a watchtower in the middle and dug out a winepress as well. 
He waited for the vineyard to yield good grapes, but the fruit it produced was sour!
“And now, O dwellers of Jerusalem and men of Judah, I exhort you to judge between Me and My vineyard.
What more could I have done for My vineyard than I already did for it? Why, when I expected sweet grapes, did it bring forth sour fruit?

Now I will tell you what I am about to do to My vineyard: 
I will take away its hedge, and it will be consumed; I will tear down its wall, and it will be trampled.

I will make it a wasteland, neither pruned nor cultivated, 
and thorns and briers will grow up. I will command the clouds that rain shall not fall on it.”

For the vineyard of the LORD of Hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah are the plant of His delight. He looked for {{mishpat2|justice}}, but saw bloodshed; for {{tzedaqah2|righteousness}}, but heard a cry of distress.
Woe to you who add house to house and join field to field until no place is left and you live alone in the land.

I heard the LORD of Hosts declare: 
“Surely many houses will become desolate, great mansions left unoccupied.
For ten acres of vineyard will yield but a bath of wine, and a homer of seed only an ephah of grain. ”
Woe to those who rise early in the morning in pursuit of strong drink, who linger into the evening, to be inflamed by wine.
At their feasts are the lyre and harp, tambourines and flutes and wine. They disregard the actions of the LORD and fail to see the work of His hands.
Therefore My people will go into exile for their lack of understanding; their dignitaries are starving and their masses are parched with thirst.
Therefore Sheol enlarges its throat and opens wide its enormous jaws, and down go Zion's nobles and masses, her revelers and carousers!
So mankind will be brought low, and each man humbled; the arrogant will lower their eyes.
But the LORD of Hosts will be exalted by His justice, and the holy God will show Himself holy in righteousness.
Lambs will graze as in their own pastures, and strangers will feed in the ruins of the wealthy.
Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of deceit and pull sin along with cart ropes,
to those who say, “Let Him hurry and hasten His work so that we may see it! Let the plan of the Holy One of Israel come so that we may know it!”
Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who turn darkness to light and light to darkness, who replace bitter with sweet and sweet with bitter.
Woe to those who are wise in their own eyes and clever in their own sight.
Woe to those who are heroes in drinking wine and champions in mixing beer,
who acquit the guilty for a bribe and deprive the innocent of justice.

Therefore, as a tongue of fire consumes the straw, and as dry grass shrivels in the flame, so their roots will decay and their blossoms will blow away like dust; for they have rejected the instruction of the LORD of Hosts and despised the word of the Holy One of Israel.

units.label: The LORD whistles for a distant nation
units.note: an army summoned like a lion

verses.text:
Therefore the anger of the LORD burns against His people; His hand is raised against them to strike them down. The mountains quake, and the corpses lay like refuse in the streets. 
Despite all this, His anger is not turned away; His hand is still upraised.

He lifts a banner for the distant nations and whistles for those at the ends of the earth. 
Behold, how speedily and swiftly they come!
None of them grows weary or stumbles; no one slumbers or sleeps. No belt is loose and no sandal strap is broken.
Their arrows are sharpened, and all their bows are strung. The hooves of their horses are like flint; their chariot wheels are like a whirlwind.
Their roaring is like that of a lion; they roar like young lions. They growl and seize their prey; they carry it away from deliverance.

In that day they will roar over it, like the roaring of the sea. If one looks over the land, he will see darkness and distress; 
even the light will be obscured by clouds.

kerem.gloss: Vineyard, a common Old Testament image for Israel as the object of God's careful cultivation and love. Everything about the vineyard here, the fertile hill, cleared stones, finest vines, watchtower, and winepress, pictures lavish, deliberate care that makes the resulting wild grapes all the more shocking.

mishpat2.gloss: Justice, the right ordering of community life. Verse 7 turns on a devastating Hebrew wordplay: God looked for mishpat, justice, but found mispach, bloodshed, sounds that are nearly identical in Hebrew, driving home how close true justice was replaced by its violent counterfeit.

tzedaqah2.gloss: Righteousness, right conduct and right relationship. The same verse plays on tzedaqah versus tze'aqah, a cry of distress, another near-rhyme in Hebrew, so the vineyard's fruit is the very opposite, in sound and substance, of what was planted.

basic.context: Isaiah sings what sounds like a love song about a vineyard lovingly prepared on a fertile hill, only to reveal partway through that the vineyard is Israel and the disappointing fruit is Judah's injustice and violence. The chapter then lists six specific woes, greed for land, drunken indifference, moral inversion, self-conceit, and corrupt courts, before ending with God summoning a foreign army as swift and relentless as a lion to execute judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real indictment of real social sins in eighth century Judah, using a beloved song form to disarm the hearers before the twist reveals they are the guilty vineyard, a rhetorical strategy similar to Nathan's parable to David in 2 Samuel 12. The uncomfortable question the chapter raises is why God would use a foreign, pagan army as His own instrument; the answer running through Isaiah is that God is sovereign over every nation and can wield even Israel's enemies for His righteous purposes.
basic.originalLanguages: God's vineyard, kerem, receives every possible advantage, yet yields wild grapes instead of good fruit. The devastating point of verse 7 is a Hebrew wordplay: God sought mishpat, justice, but found mispach, bloodshed, and looked for tzedaqah, righteousness, but heard tze'aqah, a cry of distress, near-identical sounds exposing how far the fruit had twisted from what was planted.
basic.typology: The vineyard given every advantage yet yielding wild grapes anticipates Jesus's own parable of the vineyard and the wicked tenants, Matthew 21:33-41, where the LORD of the vineyard finally sends His own Son. The lion-like army summoned from the ends of the earth foreshadows how God remains sovereign over every nation, even hostile ones, using them within His larger redemptive purposes.
basic.christ: Jesus deliberately picks up Isaiah's vineyard image in His own parable of the wicked tenants, Matthew 21:33-46, alluding directly to this song, but pushes it further: rather than merely disciplining an unfruitful vineyard, God sends His own beloved Son into it, and is rejected and killed, yet becomes the cornerstone of a new, fruitful people. Christ is also the true and faithful Israel who, unlike the vineyard here, produced the justice and righteousness the Father sought.
basic.god: God is pictured as a devoted vinedresser, doing everything possible for His people, which makes their unfaithfulness all the more inexcusable and His subsequent judgment all the more just rather than arbitrary. Yet even in summoning judgment, God remains sovereign over history's nations, showing that His justice operates on a scale far larger than Israel alone.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 12:1-7 uses a similarly disarming parable to expose David's own sin before pronouncing judgment. Psalm 80:8-16 also pictures Israel as a vine God brought out of Egypt and later broke down. Matthew 21:33-46 directly develops Isaiah's vineyard song into the parable of the wicked tenants. Habakkuk 1:5-11 likewise wrestles with God raising up a fierce foreign nation as an instrument of judgment.
basic.oneStory: The Song of the Vineyard pictures the whole story of God's patient, lavish care for His people met again and again with unfaithfulness, a pattern Jesus Himself invokes when He tells of a vineyard owner who finally sends his own son. The story's resolution is not another failed vineyard but the true Vine, Christ, who bears the fruit Israel could not, John 15:1-5.

context.literary: The chapter opens as a love song, using the language of a wedding or harvest celebration, before pivoting in verse 3 into direct address and legal appeal, then in verse 7 revealing the allegory: the vineyard is Israel, the vinedresser is the LORD. The six woes that follow function almost like an itemized indictment, each beginning with hoy, woe, before the chapter closes with an oracle summoning a foreign army in vivid, almost cinematic detail.
context.historical: Reflects the prosperity and inequality of eighth century BC Judah, likely under Uzziah or his successors, when the wealthy consolidated land at the expense of small farmers (verse 8), drunken feasting flourished among the elite (verses 11-12), and courts were bought with bribes (verse 23). The summoned army in verses 26-30 anticipates Assyria's coming campaigns, though the passage leaves the nation formally unnamed, functioning almost as a template later fulfilled by both Assyria and Babylon.
context.canonical: The vineyard as a symbol for Israel recurs throughout Scripture, Psalm 80, Jeremiah 2:21, Hosea 10:1, Ezekiel 19:10-14, before Jesus takes up the image directly in His parables and identifies Himself as the true vine in John 15:1-5, resolving the tension this song leaves unresolved.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To use the disarming form of a love song to draw hearers into agreeing with God's verdict against His own vineyard before they realize they are the ones being judged, and to itemize specific social sins, greed, drunkenness, moral confusion, pride, and corrupt courts, that justify the coming judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes specific historical sins in Judah and announces a specific, historical judgment; it is not a prescription for social policy in the abstract. The enduring principles are that God's people are accountable for the fruit their privileges are meant to produce, and that calling evil good, or justice for sale, invites real consequences.

debates.question: Is the army summoned in verses 26-30 a specific historical nation, or a more general figure for any instrument of judgment?

views.label: Assyria specifically
views.case: Given Isaiah's broader eighth century context and the later chapters naming Assyria explicitly as the LORD's rod of anger, Isaiah 10:5, many read this army as Assyria, whose disciplined, swift military campaigns match the description closely.

views.label: A deliberately unnamed, archetypal instrument
views.case: The oracle names no nation, and its vivid, almost mythic imagery, lions, whirlwind chariots, could apply to Assyria or later Babylon alike, suggesting Isaiah leaves it open as a template for whichever nation God chooses to wield.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the central point: God sovereignly commands even distant, pagan nations as instruments of His justice, a truth that does not depend on identifying the specific army by name.

study.originalLanguages: The kerem, vineyard, given every possible advantage yields wild grapes instead of good fruit, the shocking twist that turns a love song into an indictment. Verse 7 hinges on a devastating wordplay: God sought mishpat, justice, but found mispach, bloodshed, and looked for tzedaqah, righteousness, but heard tze'aqah, a cry of distress, near-identical Hebrew sounds exposing how completely the fruit had inverted from what was planted.

typology.type: The vineyard given every advantage yet bearing wild grapes, Isaiah 5:1-7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus's parable of the vineyard and the wicked tenants, where the owner finally sends his own beloved son, Matthew 21:33-41.
Christ, the true vine, who alone bears the fruit of righteousness His people could not, John 15:1-5.

study.christ: Jesus deliberately draws on Isaiah's song in His parable of the wicked tenants, Matthew 21:33-46, but pushes the story further: rather than merely fencing off and abandoning the unfruitful vineyard, the owner finally sends his own beloved son, who is killed, yet becomes the cornerstone of a new and fruitful people. Christ also fulfills what Israel failed to be, declaring Himself the true vine, John 15:1-5, who alone produces, and enables His people to produce, the justice and righteousness the Father always sought.
study.god: God is the devoted vinedresser who withheld nothing from His people, which is exactly why their unfaithfulness is so inexcusable and His judgment so evidently just rather than arbitrary or cruel. Yet His justice operates on a scale far beyond Israel alone; He remains sovereign even over the pagan nations He summons as instruments, proof that no earthly power, however fierce, moves outside His authority.

crossReferences.note: Nathan's parable of the ewe lamb uses the same disarming strategy, drawing David to condemn himself before the verdict lands.
Pictures Israel as a vine God brought out of Egypt and later broke down in judgment, the same image developed here.
Jesus directly develops Isaiah's vineyard song into the parable of the wicked tenants and the beloved son.
Jesus identifies Himself as the true vine, bearing the fruit Israel, the unfruitful vineyard, could not produce.

study.oneStory: The Song of the Vineyard pictures the whole biblical story of God's patient, lavish love for His people met repeatedly with unfaithfulness, the same pattern Jesus invokes when He tells of an owner who finally sends his own son into the vineyard. The resolution is not another failed vine but Christ, the true Vine, who bears the fruit of justice and righteousness His people could never produce on their own.

quiz.q: What is the twist revealed partway through the Song of the Vineyard?

options.text: The vineyard, lovingly prepared, is Israel and Judah, and the disappointing fruit is their injustice and violence.
options.note: Verse 7 makes the allegory explicit: the house of Israel is the LORD's vineyard.

options.text: The vineyard represents Jerusalem's physical agriculture with no symbolic meaning.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly identifies the vineyard as the house of Israel, a clear allegory.

options.text: The vineyard belongs to a foreign king, not the LORD.
options.note: The song is explicitly about 'my beloved,' God, and His own vineyard.

options.text: The vineyard produced good fruit but was destroyed anyway.
options.note: The song specifically says the vineyard produced wild, sour grapes despite every advantage.

quiz.q: What is the effect of the Hebrew wordplay in verse 7, mishpat versus mispach, tzedaqah versus tze'aqah?

options.text: It sonically underscores how completely justice was replaced by its violent, near-identical-sounding counterfeit.
options.note: The near-rhymes in Hebrew drive home the shocking reversal of what God expected versus what He found.

options.text: It shows the words are simply synonyms with no rhetorical purpose.
options.note: The pairs are opposites, justice versus bloodshed, righteousness versus distress, deliberately chosen for their similar sound.

options.text: It is a scribal error later corrected in translation.
options.note: This is a deliberate poetic device, not an error.

options.text: It only functions in English translation, not the original Hebrew.
options.note: The wordplay works specifically in the Hebrew sounds, and English translations can only describe rather than fully reproduce it.

quiz.q: What sin does the first woe, verse 8, specifically condemn?

options.text: Greedily accumulating houses and fields until small landholders are pushed out entirely.
options.note: This reflects the real economic exploitation of small farmers by the wealthy in Isaiah's day.

options.text: Refusing to own any property at all.
options.note: The woe condemns excessive accumulation, not the absence of property.

options.text: Failing to pay agricultural taxes.
options.note: The text names land consolidation and displacement of others, not tax evasion specifically.

options.text: Building too many watchtowers for defense.
options.note: The woe is about houses and fields being joined together, not military structures.

quiz.q: What does the woe in verse 20, calling evil good and good evil, describe?

options.text: A moral inversion where society's basic categories of right and wrong have been reversed.
options.note: This is one of the most quoted verses on moral relativism and confusion.

options.text: Simple disagreement about minor matters of preference.
options.note: The verse describes a fundamental inversion of moral categories, not mere preference.

options.text: A description of colorblindness.
options.note: Darkness and light, bitter and sweet, are metaphors for moral categories, not physical vision.

options.text: Praise for those who think independently.
options.note: The verse pronounces woe, judgment, not praise, on this moral inversion.

quiz.q: How does the chapter describe the army summoned in verses 26-30?

options.text: Swift, tireless, disciplined, and relentless, like a lion seizing its prey, with no one able to rescue the victims.
options.note: The vivid military imagery emphasizes the certainty and thoroughness of the coming judgment.

options.text: A weak and disorganized rabble easily repelled.
options.note: The description emphasizes overwhelming strength and precision, the opposite of weakness.

options.text: An army that will arrive slowly over many years.
options.note: Verse 26 explicitly says, 'how speedily and swiftly they come.'

options.text: A symbolic army with no real-world referent.
options.note: While the nation is left unnamed, the description matches real historical military campaigns like Assyria's, not a purely symbolic force.

questions: God did everything possible for His vineyard, yet it produced wild grapes. Where has God given you every advantage, and what fruit is your life actually producing in response?
The six woes name specific, concrete sins, greed, drunken indifference, moral confusion, pride, corrupt justice. Which of these hits closest to home for you right now, and why?
Verse 20 warns against calling evil good and good evil. Where are you tempted to relabel something wrong as acceptable because it is common or convenient?
God remained sovereign even over the pagan army He summoned as judgment. What does it mean for your own anxieties to know that no power, however fierce, operates outside God's authority?

Jesus later took up this vineyard image and said the owner finally sent his own son. How does knowing God's patience led all the way to sending Christ change the weight you give to His patience with you now?

translationNotes.note: 'My beloved' can describe a friend, a lover, or a kinsman in Hebrew; the song's opening romantic register makes the abrupt legal turn in verse 3 more jarring by contrast.

The wordplay mishpat, justice, versus mispach, bloodshed, and tzedaqah, righteousness, versus tze'aqah, a cry of distress, is one of the most celebrated examples of Hebrew paronomasia in the prophets, largely untranslatable into English directly.
Sheol is personified with a throat and jaws, vivid imagery for the grave's insatiable appetite, common in ancient Near Eastern poetic descriptions of death.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 5:1-30. Where BSB printed an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
